title: EA Governance for the Agentic Organisation
subtitle: From periodic review boards to continuous automated compliance with exception-based human oversight. How architecture governance must evolve when AI agents operate at machine speed.
description: This page synthesises the traditional governance framework with modern practices and AI-specific extensions. It covers governance fundamentals, architecture principles, review boards, compliance management, and the new governance mechanisms required for autonomous AI agents.
sources: ISO/IEC 38500 — Corporate Governance of Information Technology
COBIT 2019 — Governance and Management Objectives for Enterprise IT
EU AI Act — Regulation on Artificial Intelligence (effective August 2026)
NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
Singapore IMDA Model AI Governance Framework for Agentic AI (January 2026)
O'Reilly — How Agentic AI Empowers Architecture Governance (2025)

sections.label: Governance Fundamentals
sections.summary: EA governance is the practice by which enterprise architectures are managed and controlled — setting direction, defining decision rights, and ensuring compliance. It is distinct from EA management: governance asks 'are we doing the right things?' while management asks 'are we doing things right?'

subsections.title: Governance vs. Management
subsections.content:
AspectGovernanceManagement
FocusDirection-setting, oversight, controlDay-to-day execution and delivery
Core question"Are we doing the right things?""Are we doing things right?"
ActivitiesSetting policy, defining decision rights, ensuring complianceCreating architectures, running processes, delivering artefacts
CadenceStrategic reviews, exception handlingContinuous operational activity
AccountabilityBoard-level / C-suiteArchitecture practice leads

subsections.title: Decision Rights
subsections.content:
Decision rights define who has authority to make which architectural decisions. A well-defined model uses RACI — Responsible (executes), Accountable (owns the outcome), Consulted (provides input), Informed (receives updates).

Decision TypeAccountableResponsibleConsulted
Enterprise-wide technology standardsCIO/CTOChief Enterprise ArchitectDomain architects, security, procurement
Domain architecture decisionsDomain Architecture LeadDomain/Solution ArchitectsEnterprise architect, business stakeholders
Solution architecture (within standards)Solution ArchitectDevelopment leadDomain architect, security
Architecture exceptions/waiversArchitecture BoardRequesting teamEnterprise architect, risk
Strategic technology directionCIO/CTOEnterprise Architecture teamBusiness leadership, innovation

subsections.title: Governance Hierarchy
subsections.content:
EA governance operates within a four-level hierarchy:

  1. Corporate governance — Board-level oversight of enterprise direction
  2. Technology governance — CIO/CTO oversight of technology strategy and investment
  3. IT governance — Management of IT operations, services, and delivery
  4. Architecture governance — Control of architectural decisions, standards, and compliance

The Architecture Board sits as the primary governance body, with authority delegated downward to design authorities, working parties, and domain-specific groups.

subsections.title: Six Characteristics of Good Governance
subsections.content:
Discipline

All parties commit to agreed procedures, processes, and authority structures

Transparency

Decisions are open and documented; rationale is visible to all stakeholders

Independence

Mechanisms exist to minimise conflicts of interest

Accountability

Identifiable groups are authorised and accountable for their actions

Responsibility

Each party acts responsibly to the organisation and stakeholders

Fairness

Decisions do not create unfair advantage; processes apply equally

These six characteristics are arguably more important for governing autonomous agents than for governing human-led projects. They should be preserved and elevated as the durable contribution of traditional governance thinking.

sections.label: Governance Frameworks
sections.summary: Multiple frameworks address EA governance at different levels. The practical approach is layered governance: ISO 38500 for board-level principles, COBIT for operational controls, EA frameworks for architecture method, with AI-specific frameworks layered on top.

subsections.title: EA Governance Framework
subsections.content:
EA governance separates into three pillars — a content-agnostic design that allows new governance material (e.g., AI agent policies) to be introduced without redesigning processes:

— a content-agnostic design that allows new governance material (e.g., AI agent policies) to be introduced without redesigning processes:

Context

Business, technology, and regulatory drivers that shape governance requirements

Process

Governance workflows: policy management, compliance assessment, dispensation handling, monitoring

Content

The governed material: principles, policies, standards, reference architectures, patterns

Key governance components: Architecture Board, Architecture Compliance Reviews, Architecture Contracts, Architecture Principles, and the Dispensation (waiver) process.

subsections.title: Framework Integration
subsections.content:
LayerFrameworkPurpose
Board principlesISO 38500Strategic direction via Evaluate-Direct-Monitor model
Controls & processCOBIT 2019Operational governance — 5 domains, 40 management objectives
Architecture methodEA FrameworksDesign governance, compliance reviews, standards management
Service managementITILOperational service governance
SecurityISO 27001 / NISTSecurity-specific controls and frameworks
AI-specificEU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001AI risk classification, compliance, and management

subsections.title: What Changes for Agentic Organisations
subsections.content:
The traditional governance framework is structurally sound but operationally misaligned with AI deployment velocity:

What Breaks
  • Meeting-cadence governance — A board that meets monthly to evaluate architectures designed three weeks ago and deployed next Tuesday is performing governance theatre
  • Milestone-based compliance — AI model lifecycles are months, not years; waiting for milestone reviews means non-compliant deployments operate unchecked
  • Deterministic risk models — Agent cascade failures, hallucination, and emergent multi-agent behaviours are not classifiable on standard risk matrices
  • Human-only contracts — Agent SLAs need to be machine-readable and automatically enforced
What Survives
  • Six governance characteristics — Discipline, transparency, independence, accountability, responsibility, fairness are more important than ever
  • Content-agnostic framework — New AI governance content slots in without process redesign
  • Multi-level structure — Global boards, local boards, design authorities scale naturally
  • Dispensation process — Directionally correct; needs automated fast-track for pre-defined categories

sections.label: Architecture Principles
sections.summary: Architecture principles are general rules and guidelines intended to be enduring and seldom amended. Each principle has a name, statement, rationale, and implications. These 12 principles are designed for organisations operating in an AI-augmented environment.

principles.title: Business Alignment First
principles.statement: Every technology decision must demonstrably support a stated business objective. No architecture investment proceeds without a clear link to business value.
principles.rationale: Technology decisions made in isolation from business context create solutions that are technically elegant but organisationally irrelevant. Alignment ensures resources flow to initiatives that advance the enterprise mission.
implications: Architecture proposals must include a business case linking to strategic objectives
Review boards evaluate proposals on business impact, not just technical merit
Architecture roadmaps are synchronised with business planning cycles
Technology investments are deprioritised when business priorities shift
principles.example: The UK Department for Education requires all architecture work to align with departmental goals, with delivery metrics linked to business objectives.

principles.title: API-First Integration
principles.statement: All business capabilities shall be exposed through well-defined, versioned APIs as the primary integration mechanism. Point-to-point integrations are not permitted for new systems.
principles.rationale: APIs create discoverable, reusable interfaces that enable rapid composition of new capabilities — essential when AI agents need to invoke business services programmatically.
implications: Every service must have a documented API contract before implementation
API design standards (naming, versioning, error handling) are mandatory
An API gateway provides centralised authentication and rate limiting
Agent-to-service and agent-to-agent interactions use the same API standards
principles.example: NHS England mandates API-first design for all health and social care digital services, enabling interoperability across trusts and providers.

principles.title: Cloud-Native by Default
principles.statement: New workloads shall be designed for cloud-native deployment using containers, managed services, and infrastructure-as-code. On-premises deployment requires documented justification.
principles.rationale: Cloud-native architectures provide the elasticity, resilience, and operational automation that AI workloads demand. Container orchestration reduces operational overhead and enables rapid scaling.
implications: Teams must justify on-premises deployment with regulatory or technical rationale
Applications are designed for horizontal scaling, statelessness, and graceful degradation
Infrastructure is defined as code and deployed through CI/CD pipelines
Hybrid deployment is acceptable where requirements dictate
principles.example: Cloudera recommends a 'workload-first' approach — public cloud for bursty training, on-premises for high-volume production inference, edge for low-latency decisions.

principles.title: Data as a Shared Enterprise Asset
principles.statement: Data is an enterprise asset, not a departmental possession. Data must be discoverable, well-governed, and accessible through standard interfaces to all authorised consumers, including AI agents.
principles.rationale: AI agents are only as effective as the data they can access. Siloed data creates blind spots, forces duplication, and prevents AI systems from making well-informed decisions.
implications: Every data asset has an identified owner, steward, and published quality SLA
Master data is managed through authoritative sources accessed via APIs
Data classification, lineage tracking, and quality controls are mandatory
AI training data is catalogued with provenance and bias assessments
principles.example: The UK DfE principle requires maintaining accurate, consistent, and reliable data using authoritative master data sources and shared interfaces.

principles.title: Reuse Before Build
principles.statement: Before building new capability, teams must demonstrate they have searched for existing solutions — first within the enterprise, then COTS, then open source. Bespoke development is a last resort.
principles.rationale: Duplication wastes resources, increases maintenance burden, and creates inconsistency. Reuse builds a composable architecture where AI agents can assemble capabilities from existing services.
implications: A searchable catalogue of reusable services, APIs, and components is maintained
Architecture reviews verify that reuse options were evaluated before approving new builds
Components are designed for reuse from the outset
'Good enough' COTS solutions are preferred over bespoke perfection
principles.example: The UK DfE principle: 'by decomposing services into small reusable components, teams can consume these rather than building functionality from scratch.'

principles.title: Security by Design
principles.statement: Security and privacy requirements are incorporated into every phase of solution design, from concept to decommissioning. Security is not a gate at the end — it is woven in from day one.
principles.rationale: When AI agents operate autonomously, security flaws can be exploited at machine speed. Early integration reduces risk, protects data, and avoids costly remediation.
implications: Threat modelling is conducted early and continuously
All data is classified, with encryption policies enforced based on classification
Agent identity and authorisation follow zero-trust principles
Security testing (SAST, DAST, penetration) is automated in CI/CD pipelines
principles.example: Palo Alto Networks recommends unified identity management across people, services, and agents, with least-privilege access and expiring credentials.

principles.title: Loosely Coupled Architecture
principles.statement: Systems shall be composed of independent components with well-defined interfaces and minimal knowledge of each other's internal implementation.
principles.rationale: Loose coupling enables independent deployment, testing, and scaling — essential for agent-based architectures where capabilities are composed dynamically. It reduces blast radius when failures occur.
implications: Services communicate through APIs, events, or message queues — never shared databases
Each service owns its data and exposes it through published contracts
Breaking changes follow a versioning and deprecation policy
Agent architectures can compose capabilities without tight coupling
principles.example: DORA research shows loosely coupled teams achieve higher deployment frequency, lower change failure rate, and faster time to recover from incidents.

principles.title: Automation Over Manual Process
principles.statement: Repeatable processes — testing, deployment, compliance checking, monitoring — must be automated. Manual processes are permitted only for genuinely judgment-dependent activities.
principles.rationale: Manual processes do not scale, introduce human error, and create bottlenecks. When AI agents are part of the enterprise, governance and operations must operate at machine speed.
implications: CI/CD pipelines automate build, test, and deployment for all production systems
Infrastructure is provisioned through code (Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation)
Compliance checks are automated in deployment pipelines (policy-as-code)
Governance uses automated pre-screening with human review by exception
principles.example: GitOps adoption reached two-thirds of organisations by 2025, with 80%+ reporting higher infrastructure reliability and faster rollbacks.

principles.title: Design for Observability
principles.statement: Every system must emit structured logs, metrics, and traces that enable real-time understanding of behaviour, performance, and health without requiring code changes.
principles.rationale: Complex systems with non-deterministic AI agents cannot be governed by periodic audits alone. Real-time observability is the foundation for continuous compliance and incident response.
implications: Every service implements structured logging, distributed tracing, and metric emission
Agent actions are logged with sufficient detail for behaviour analysis and audit
Dashboards provide real-time visibility into system health and agent behaviour
Observability is a deployment prerequisite — services without it are not production-ready
principles.example: Fiddler AI's Control Plane provides 'standardised telemetry, reliable evaluation, continuous monitoring, enforceable policy, and auditable governance across the AI agent lifecycle.'

principles.title: Technology Agnosticism
principles.statement: Architecture decisions prioritise open standards, portable formats, and multi-vendor compatibility. Solutions must not create irreversible dependency on a single vendor.
principles.rationale: AI technology is evolving rapidly — today's leading platform may be obsolete within two years. Portability preserves the ability to adopt superior solutions as they emerge.
implications: Containerisation and standard APIs are preferred over proprietary integrations
Data is stored in open formats with documented export capabilities
Multi-cloud and hybrid deployment must be architecturally feasible
AI models support framework portability (ONNX, standard serving formats)
principles.example: Organisations increasingly adopt Kubernetes and service mesh technologies to maintain portability across cloud providers.

principles.title: Design for Resilience
principles.statement: Systems shall continue operating in degraded mode when components fail. No single point of failure shall exist in production architectures for business-critical services.
principles.rationale: AI agents operating autonomously amplify the impact of failures — a failing agent can make thousands of bad decisions before a human notices. Resilience patterns limit blast radius.
implications: Critical services implement redundancy, failover, and circuit-breaker patterns
Chaos engineering validates resilience assumptions in production
Agent systems include kill switches and fallback modes
Dependent service failures trigger graceful degradation, not cascading failure
principles.example: Netflix pioneered chaos engineering with Chaos Monkey, demonstrating that deliberately failing components in production builds more resilient systems.

principles.title: Responsible AI by Default
principles.statement: AI systems are designed, deployed, and operated with fairness, transparency, explainability, and accountability as non-negotiable requirements. Every AI system has an accountable human owner.
principles.rationale: AI agents making autonomous decisions create new categories of risk that traditional governance was not designed to address. Regulations like the EU AI Act enforce mandatory risk assessments for high-risk AI systems.
implications: Every AI system has a documented risk classification and an accountable human owner
Model decisions are explainable to the degree required by use case and regulation
Bias testing is automated in training and deployment pipelines
Human override mechanisms are mandatory for high-stakes decision domains
principles.example: Singapore's IMDA Framework for Agentic AI (January 2026) requires clear human accountability across the AI lifecycle — developers, deployers, operators, and end users.

sections.label: Architecture Review Boards
sections.summary: The Architecture Review Board is a cross-functional governance body that reviews, approves, and monitors architectural decisions. 93% of CIOs admit their organisations are not equipped to make fast, well-informed technology decisions — a well-functioning ARB addresses this.

subsections.title: Board Composition
subsections.content:
A typical ARB includes 4-10 members, balanced between technical expertise and business acumen:

RoleResponsibility
Chief Enterprise Architect (Chair)Leads meetings, ensures agenda coverage, drives decisions
CTO / CIO (Sponsor)Executive sponsorship, strategic alignment, authority
Domain Architects (2-3)Deep technical expertise in business, data, application, technology
CISO / Security ArchitectSecurity and risk assessment for all proposals
Data Officer / Data ArchitectData governance, privacy, and data architecture alignment
Business Unit RepresentativeBusiness context, value assessment, stakeholder perspective
Operations / SRE LeadOperational readiness, supportability, resilience
AI/ML Architect (new role)Model governance, agent architecture, non-deterministic system risk

Staggered terms (typically 2 years) maintain continuity while refreshing perspectives. Rotating guest seats bring in subject-matter experts relevant to agenda items.

subsections.title: Types of Review Boards
subsections.content:
Board TypeScopeCadenceFocus
Enterprise Architecture BoardEnterprise-wideMonthly or quarterlyStrategic direction, cross-cutting standards, major exceptions
Domain Design AuthoritySingle domainFortnightlyDomain-specific standards, pattern approval, tactical guidance
Solution ReviewIndividual projectsPer milestoneSpecific solution compliance, risk assessment
Agent Architecture Working Party (new)AI/Agent deploymentsWeekly or fortnightlyAgent deployment reviews, guardrail validation, model governance

subsections.title: Decision-Making Model
subsections.content:
Decisions follow a structured outcome model:

Approved

Proceeds as designed

Approved with Conditions

Proceeds with specified modifications or additional controls

Deferred

Requires additional information or analysis

Rejected

Does not meet standards; alternative approach required

Every decision is documented with justification and becomes reference precedent. Pre-meeting preparation is essential — every proposal should include: architecture overview, standards compliance assessment, risk assessment, cost-benefit analysis, and reuse assessment.

subsections.title: Making ARBs Effective
subsections.content:
• Define scope clearly — geographic span, domain focus, and which projects require review
  • Establish predefined rules of engagement — not every project needs full board review
  • Use checklists to standardise evaluations and ensure consistency
  • Maintain a centralised repository as a single source of truth for standards and decisions
  • Embed in Agile practices — align review cadence with sprint and programme increments
  • Measure effectiveness — track cycle time, exception rates, decision quality, technical debt reduction
  • Reduce friction — the most valuable outcome is trust in the technology process, eliminating shadow IT

sections.label: Compliance & Standards
sections.summary: Architecture compliance ensures projects conform to established standards. Traditional EA defines a spectrum from irrelevant to fully conformant, with a formal dispensation process for managing exceptions.

subsections.title: Compliance Levels
subsections.content:
LevelDescription
IrrelevantStandard does not apply to this project
ConsistentImplementation has no conflicts with the architecture specification
CompliantImplementation is in accordance with the architecture standard
ConformantImplementation is fully in accordance with the architecture specification
Fully ConformantAll specifications are met, and all standards are adhered to
Non-conformantSome specifications or standards are not met

subsections.title: Compliance Assessment Process
subsections.content:
1. Trigger — Project milestone, change request, or deployment gate
  2. Scope — Identify which standards and principles apply
  3. Evidence — Architecture documentation, design decisions, implementation details
  4. Review — Formal assessment against applicable standards
  5. Outcome — Compliance level determination with findings
  6. Action — Remediation plan for non-conformant items, or dispensation request
  7. Tracking — Open items tracked to resolution

subsections.title: Dispensation (Waiver) Process
subsections.content:
Where compliance assessment identifies non-conformance, the dispensation process provides a formal mechanism for time-bound exceptions.

When Dispensation Is Needed
  • Remediation cost exceeds 15% of project value or USD 0.5 million
  • The exception introduces technology not currently approved or classified as 'Emerging'
  • The exception violates an architecture principle
  • The technology is identified for 'Containment' or 'Retirement'
Request Requirements
  • Business justification and impact of not granting the exception
  • Technical merit of the proposed approach
  • Collateral impact to other systems and processes
  • Alternatives evaluated and reasons for rejection
  • Proposed duration and exit strategy
  • Risk assessment and mitigations
  • Precedent-setting implications

subsections.title: Governance Metrics
subsections.content:
Compliance Rate

Percentage of projects that conform to architecture standards

Exception Rate

Number and severity of dispensation requests over time

Governance Cycle Time

Elapsed time from review request to approved decision

Time to Resolution

How quickly non-conformance is addressed

Technical Debt

Accumulated impact of dispensations and exceptions

Architecture Maturity

Score based on ACMM, Gartner ITScore, or similar

sections.label: AI & Agent Governance
sections.summary: Traditional governance was designed for human-initiated, milestone-based projects. AI agents introduce non-determinism, autonomy, velocity, chaining, and continuous learning — fundamentally different characteristics that require new governance mechanisms.

subsections.title: The Governance Gap
subsections.content:
Organisations with mature AI governance frameworks experience 23% fewer AI-related incidents and achieve 31% faster time-to-market for new AI capabilities. Traditional governance fails for agents because:

  • Non-determinism — The same input may produce different outputs
  • Autonomy — Agents act without per-action human approval
  • Velocity — Agents make thousands of decisions per minute
  • Chaining — Multi-agent systems create emergent behaviour
  • Continuous learning — Model behaviour drifts over time

subsections.title: Regulatory Landscape (2025-2026)
subsections.content:
RegulationScopeKey Requirements
EU AI ActAll AI systems in EU (high-risk: Aug 2026)Risk classification, transparency, human oversight, documentation, logging
NIST AI RMFUS voluntary frameworkGovern, Map, Measure, Manage approach to AI risk
ISO/IEC 42001International standardAI management system requirements — establishment, implementation, maintenance
Singapore IMDAFirst agentic AI frameworkRisk bounding, human accountability, technical controls, end-user responsibility

subsections.title: Policy-as-Code
subsections.content:
Policy-as-code translates governance rules into machine-executable code, replacing 100-page PDF compliance documents with automatically enforced policies.

Implementation Patterns
  • Centralised orchestration — All decisions flow through a single control point. Ensures consistency but can create latency.
  • Distributed enforcement — Governance engines embedded within agents enable autonomous operation and instant decisions.
  • Hybrid governance — Core compliance rules centralised, localised adaptations for specific environments or regions.
Key Tools
  • Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Rego — the industry standard for policy-as-code
  • AWS Cedar — purpose-built policy language
  • Kyverno — Kubernetes-native policy management
  • Styra DAS — commercial OPA-based governance platform

Gartner predicts by 2026, 70% of enterprises will integrate compliance-as-code into DevOps toolchains, reducing risk management overhead by at least 15%. Organisations adopting policy-as-code typically see 40-70% reduction in compliance costs.

subsections.title: Agent Deployment Approval Workflow
subsections.content:
1. Registration — Agent registered in enterprise agent registry with owner, purpose, data access, and authority scope
  2. Risk Classification — Classified by autonomy level, data sensitivity, and decision impact
  3. Design Review — Architecture review appropriate to risk tier:
    • Low risk: Self-service compliance check against automated guardrails
    • Medium risk: Design authority review
    • High risk: Full Architecture Board review with human-in-the-loop
  4. Pre-production Validation — Automated testing against bias, performance, and safety benchmarks
  5. Deployment Gate — Policy-as-code checks validate guardrails, monitoring, and escalation paths
  6. Production Monitoring — Continuous runtime monitoring with behavioural anomaly detection
  7. Periodic Re-certification — Regular review to detect drift and reassess risk
  8. Retirement — Formal decommissioning with data retention and handover

subsections.title: Continuous Monitoring vs. Milestone Reviews
subsections.content:
AspectMilestone ReviewsContinuous Monitoring
CadenceQuarterly, annual, or per-phaseReal-time, continuous
ScopeDesign-time complianceRuntime behaviour and compliance
DetectionIssues found at next reviewIssues detected within minutes
ResponseRemediation planned for next quarterAutomated intervention or immediate escalation
EvidencePoint-in-time snapshotsContinuous audit trail
For AIInadequate — agents drift between reviewsDesigned for non-deterministic systems

The shift: From "review and approve" to "monitor and intervene." Milestone reviews remain valuable for strategic decisions, but continuous monitoring is required for operational AI governance.

sections.label: Governance Anti-Patterns
sections.summary: Recognising dysfunctional governance patterns is as important as implementing good ones. These eight anti-patterns represent the most common governance failures observed in practice.

anti_patterns.title: The Ivory Tower
anti_patterns.symptoms: Architecture team produces polished frameworks that delivery teams ignore. No regular engagement with project teams. Architects cannot explain how their guidance helps solve real problems.
anti_patterns.root_cause: Architecture positioned as a strategic function disconnected from delivery.
anti_patterns.impact: In 2023, only 35% of professionals said architects add value. By 2025, that rose to 47% — but only where architects actively engage in delivery.
anti_patterns.fix: Architects work embedded in delivery teams at least 50% of their time. Governance focuses on enabling delivery, not controlling it.

anti_patterns.title: Design by Committee
anti_patterns.symptoms: Endless architecture meetings that end with 'let's run another spike.' Everyone has input; nobody has authority.
anti_patterns.root_cause: Unclear decision rights. Too many people need to agree but nobody owns the final call.
anti_patterns.impact: Decision-making becomes negotiation rather than discipline. Delivery slows as meeting load increases.
anti_patterns.fix: Clear RACI matrix for every decision type. Architecture decisions have a single accountable owner. Meetings have defined decision outcomes.

anti_patterns.title: Decision Drift
anti_patterns.symptoms: Teams start coding without a decision. Decisions drag because nobody has authority. Integration layers become overloaded because data ownership was never decided.
anti_patterns.root_cause: Governance processes too slow or absent. No defined escalation path when decisions stall.
anti_patterns.impact: Architectural debt accumulates silently. Rework costs escalate as undecided issues are resolved ad hoc.
anti_patterns.fix: Every decision has a defined owner and a time-boxed window. Decisions that exceed the window auto-escalate. ADRs capture decisions immediately.

anti_patterns.title: Governance Theatre
anti_patterns.symptoms: Architecture Board reviews architectures already deployed. Reviews focus on documentation completeness rather than quality. Dispensations are always granted.
anti_patterns.root_cause: Governance inserted at the wrong point in the lifecycle. No consequences for non-compliance.
anti_patterns.impact: Governance becomes a tax on delivery with no value return. Teams learn to game the process.
anti_patterns.fix: Move governance gates to design-time. Automated compliance in deployment pipelines. Dispensations require genuine business justification and time-bound remediation.

anti_patterns.title: Bureaucratic Bottleneck
anti_patterns.symptoms: All decisions queue for the same board regardless of complexity. Simple deployments wait weeks for approval. Teams work around governance, creating shadow IT.
anti_patterns.impact: Governance becomes the slowest link in the delivery chain. Shadow IT proliferates.
anti_patterns.fix: Tiered decision authority — automated approval for compliant changes, design authority for flagged issues, board escalation for strategic decisions.

anti_patterns.title: Standards Without Enforcement
anti_patterns.symptoms: Architecture standards exist but nobody checks compliance. Standards are published and forgotten. Technical debt accumulates.
anti_patterns.root_cause: No compliance review process. No integration between standards and delivery pipelines.
anti_patterns.impact: Standards become aspirational documents. Architecture diverges from intent.
anti_patterns.fix: Standards expressed as automated checks (policy-as-code). Compliance metrics reported and visible. Non-compliance has real consequences.

anti_patterns.title: Analysis Paralysis
anti_patterns.symptoms: Governance requires exhaustive documentation before any work begins. Architecture teams spend months producing target state architectures before delivery starts.
anti_patterns.root_cause: Governance designed for waterfall applied to iterative delivery.
anti_patterns.impact: 'Perfect' becomes the enemy of 'good enough.' Delivery is delayed by over-analysis.
anti_patterns.fix: Apply 'just enough' architecture — the Pareto rule (20% of functionality delivers 80% of value). Architecture evolves iteratively alongside delivery.

anti_patterns.title: Technology-Only Governance
anti_patterns.symptoms: Governance focuses exclusively on technology decisions while ignoring business architecture, data, and organisational impacts.
anti_patterns.root_cause: Architecture governance positioned within IT without business stakeholder involvement.
anti_patterns.impact: Technology decisions are made in isolation. Business architecture gaps go undetected.
anti_patterns.fix: Governance scope includes business capability impacts, data implications, and organisational readiness. Business representatives are permanent board members.

sections.label: Modern Governance Practices
sections.summary: Modern governance practices from 2024-2026 shift the paradigm from document-heavy, meeting-based governance to lightweight, automated, and continuous approaches that scale with delivery velocity.

subsections.title: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
subsections.content:
ADRs capture a single design decision in a lightweight, structured format:

# ADR-NNN: Title

## Status
[Proposed | Accepted | Deprecated | Superseded by ADR-NNN]

## Context
What is the issue motivating this decision?

## Decision
What is the change we're proposing?

## Consequences
What becomes easier or more difficult?

## AI-Specific Considerations (optional)
- Model risk classification:
- Data sensitivity:
- Human oversight requirements:
- Monitoring and drift detection:
- Regulatory implications (EU AI Act category):
ADR Governance Tiers
  • Team-level decisions — Created and accepted by the team
  • Cross-team decisions — Require design authority review
  • Enterprise-wide decisions — Require Architecture Board approval

Recommended by UK Government Digital Service, Azure Well-Architected Framework, and AWS Prescriptive Guidance.

subsections.title: Governance-as-Code
subsections.content:
Governance-as-code extends infrastructure-as-code principles to architectural governance:

  • Standards as code — Architecture standards expressed as machine-executable policies (OPA/Rego)
  • Reviews as automation — Compliance checks run in CI/CD pipelines, not in meetings
  • Decisions as data — Architecture decisions stored in structured, queryable formats
  • Enforcement as infrastructure — Policy engines enforce standards at runtime
GitOps-Style Governance Flow
  1. Architect proposes a standard change via pull request to the policy repository
  2. Automated tests validate the policy is syntactically correct and non-conflicting
  3. Peer review by domain architects and affected stakeholders
  4. Merge triggers automatic deployment of updated policies to enforcement engines
  5. All deployments are automatically evaluated against updated policies
  6. Critical violations block deployment; non-critical are reported

subsections.title: Self-Service Compliance
subsections.content:
Platform engineering and internal developer platforms enable compliance that scales:

  • Service catalogues (e.g., Backstage) — Developers select from pre-approved templates that are compliant by construction
  • Golden paths — Paved roads that make the compliant thing the easy thing
  • Automated compliance checks — Instant feedback without waiting for a review board
  • Self-assessment tools — Teams run compliance checks against their own architectures

The goal: Make compliance the path of least resistance, not an obstacle to work around.

subsections.title: Lightweight Governance for Agile Delivery
subsections.content:
Modern governance is "lightweight and traceable: intake, triage, short review, clear decision, recorded ADR, then delivery and measurement."

Risk-Based Triage
Risk TierReview TypeSLA
Low riskSelf-service compliance with automated checksSame day
Medium riskPeer review by domain architect3 business days
High riskArchitecture Board review2 weeks
Key Principles
  • Time-boxed reviews with defined SLAs
  • Every decision recorded as an ADR regardless of tier
  • Effectiveness measured by outcomes (technical debt, incident rates, delivery velocity) not process metrics (reviews completed, documents produced)

subsections.title: Architecture-as-Code & Fitness Functions
subsections.content:
The combination of fitness function libraries, the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and agentic AI enables automated governance that is both powerful and flexible:

  • Fitness functions — Automated checks that validate architectural characteristics (like unit tests for architecture)
  • MCP as an anticorruption layer — Enterprise architects state governance intent without coupling to implementation details
  • Agentic AI enforcement — AI agents interpret governance intent and validate compliance without brittle hard-coded checks

This approach lets architects express governance policies at a strategic level while allowing implementation teams flexibility in how they achieve compliance — solving the traditional brittleness problem of automated governance.
